title: 8. On Anthropological Approaches to Anomalous Phenomena: Explorations in the Science of Magic and the Narrative Structure of Paranormal Experiences: —David Metcalfe
text: 8 ON ANTHROPOLOGICAL APPROACHES TO ANOMALOUS PHENOMENA: EXPLORATIONS IN THE SCIENCE OF MAGIC AND THE NARRATIVE STRUCTURE OF PARANORMAL EXPERIENCES DAVID METCALFE In theory, science should welcome anomalies as the harbingers of new discoveries. The scientific intelligence should derive joy from being surprised by new and hitherto unexplained phenomena. After all, isn’t that what science is all about? —Joseph M. Felser, Outsiders, Anomalies, and the Future of “Forbidden Science” ALONG THE ROAD THERE CAME A STRANGER The twentieth century’s iconic Fortean adventurer, John Keel, had been writing about UFOs since 1945 as a journalist for various publications, but it was while inquiring into a series of anomalous sightings in Point Pleasant, West Virginia, in the 1970s that he found himself beyond pale reality with visions, voices, and high strangeness penetrating the virgin veil of polite familiarity. Keel might not make it onto an academic list of anthropologists, but like any true correspondent, his means and methodology were no different than those of a good ethnographer. He stepped into the reality of his investigations as a journalist willing to experience his subject matter head on. Rather than assuming he already knew, he sought out experience. In earlier work, such as the fieldwork he did in North Africa for his first book, Jaboo , he experienced foreign cultures with the intensity of an integrated outsider, and when Forteana came calling, he was no less willing to enter the role of participant observer. Quite vocal on how often these experiences are attended by fraud or foul play, he was still, to his dying day, a believer. Keel knew that the narrative structure of anomalous activity is often more important than the scientific veracity of its evidence. In exploring stories of unexplained events, the interplay of coincidence and phenomena can develop far beyond what any rational theory can hold on to. For the small price of a verifiable reason, Keel drank deep from the well of weirdness and found that common daylight does not illuminate every fold in space and time. INEFFICIENT TRUTHS Those enmeshed in corporations and economic entities like to have their schedules set and secure as they sew the seams of our daily lives. Our society’s legal and judicial branches enjoy clarity so linguistically profuse it’s nearly incomprehensible. Commoditized science prefers things logically sound, with results reducible to future funding. The narrative nature of unexplained phenomena is not easily dealt with when every functional facet of our society finds itself seeking efficiency at all cost. Stories ramble and wind their way toward uncertain conclusions, the part that can be told ends where words fail to follow, where the movement that sparked the speech slides beyond time. Anomalous phenomena break the façade of linear inquiry, eschewing sound ratios for absurd angles. Mundane science falters when experience is not explained by facts. It’s difficult to secure funding to study slippery synchronicities, especially when the results that do surface are not easily monetized. There is no measurable rationale for some stories, and yet they deeply affect how we live our lives, and how we understand and correlate events that we find ourselves party to on our journey. Sometimes stories get a bit odd: John Keel arrives on a West Virginia porch during a rainy night to ask directions and gets mistaken for the devil himself, come like a black dog to announce some future doom; and a few months later a bridge in town collapses killing over three dozen people. TRANSPERSONAL ANTHROPOLOGY Paranthropology 1 has emerged as a way to look at these phenomena through their cultural and personal evidence, a fluid yet rational approach that focuses on experiential and sensual relationships rather than seeking to secure a complex causal theory for the materialization of mysteries. It is a specialized version of what the anthropologist Charles Laughlin calls “transpersonal anthropology,” 2 explicitly looking at events and experiences that fall under the purview of what is commonly called paranormal. It is anthropology as seen through Hecate’s mirror. Jack Hunter, who runs the Journal of Anthropological Approaches to the Paranormal , did a study of Spiritualism for his thesis. Rather than just look at the movement’s history or conduct a sociological survey, he actually went in and trained with Spiritualist groups in order to enter a mediumistic trance state. 3 This kind of integrative approach is not common in a field harried by virulent skeptics quick to call undo prejudice on any investigator willing to suspend his or her assumptions long enough to enter the liminal world. Using the methods of ethnography to explore anomalous phenomena is not part of the regular procedures of parapsychology. Since the late 1960s and early 1970s, parapsychology has sought objective verification for phenomena in the laboratory rather than cultivating the experience as a participant in the field. The change in methodology that paranthropology presents mirrors approaches that have been successful for researchers exploring indigenous societies and social groups, especially in the areas of ritual and magic. Anthropology has long been the home of fringe experience. Researchers encountering other cultures are met with belief systems and experiences that run counter to their own expectations. Ethnography and immersion have proven the most successful means for overcoming this, by allowing researchers to fully explore a culture from within its own understanding. Bias is a given and provides no ground to dismiss the reality of the complex interplay of elements when the storytellers themselves enter the story. The power of this method can be seen in the resurgence of interest in the use of psychedelics for therapeutic means. What Western science failed to embrace in the laboratory fifty years ago has been forced into recognition by anthropologists and researchers who have consistently shown that psychedelics play a major role in the cohesion of many indigenous societies and, to the embarrassment of fundamentalists of any persuasion, by successful leaders who have confessed that some of their greatest insights have come while under the influence of a psychedelic experience or substance. WHAT HAS BEEN UNMOORED The history of contemporary psychedelic science shows the dangers of trying to monetize something that is, by nature, destabilizing. Neuroreactive compounds, whether imaginal or material, tear apart a person’s set patterns. When national intelligence agencies poured funding into psychedelic research hoping for the key to mind control, they found instead that the deeper your dive, the more control factors break down. The journey inward is equally as abyssal as imagining the fantastic voids at the very edge of celestial space. As an initiatory element, this destabilization is useful in ritual contexts where new elements are introduced to replace what has been unmoored, where symbolic and allegorical patterns create new pathways and relationships, but without this ritual setting the results are not as predictable. When the symbolic and allegorical patterns in the environment have been manipulated or presented to reflect dubious motives, such as an environment whose aesthetics are determined by marketing firms and corporations, the results can be terrifying or even psychosomatically damaging. Anomalous phenomena and what are commonly known as paranormal encounters demonstrate similar characteristics of destabilization. Confrontations with situations that violate our routine programming literally crack the imaginal structure that keeps us moored and secure to commercial reality. AS I OPENED THE DOOR As I opened the door, Alex (Tanous) smiled and extended his hand. Suddenly I froze, remembering that this psychic was known for something else—an uncanny ability to foretell death. He once told me he got his most accurate death impressions when he shook somebody’s hand. Hey! I thought, I don’t want to shake this guy’s hand. Instead I threw my arms around him, and we embraced. And as we embraced, I thought to myself in rapid succession—“I don’t want to believe Alex can do any of these things!” And then, “I don’t believe he can! —Michael Grosso, “Nine Reasons to Fear the Paranormal” 4 Jacques Vallee and other scientists taking a reasoned approach to studying the unexplained have shown that experiences with the unknown are often traumatizing and life changing. To move from the daylight world of objective experience into the twilight world of the unexplained can be deeply disturbing. It leaves one without an objective measure for reality, puts one outside the boundaries of what is easily explainable, and can make one an object of ridicule and rejection. The original methodology of the Society for Psychical Research used the tools of anthropology and philosophy more often than it did those of psychology or the hard sciences. The journals from the late nineteenth century are comprised mostly of recounted tales, second-hand information, metaphysical speculation, and book reviews. What hard science there is amounts to photographic evidence and specialists in the art of illusion and sleight of hand debunking or affirming the legitimacy of mediums. The story always remained at the forefront of their investigation, because the story was the motivating factor to the phenomena. The professional illusionist is perceived as an essential tool of the skeptic, yet in the same way that we miss something by calling the element of individuation in ethnography “bias,” when the illusionist is not given full breath to do more than bust hoaxes, we lose a valuable means of deepening our relationship with reality. What does it mean that even the most debased televangelists can be credited as a vehicle of liberation for some of those they pretend to heal? UNANTICIPATED SIDE EFFECTS George Hansen, 5 a former associate at the Rhine Research Center in Durham, North Carolina, has written extensively on the consistent struggle in the world of professional parapsychology to maintain credibility in the face of phenomenal experience. Unanticipated side effects attend the research when results are achieved or seem close to being achieved; things start to happen that destabilize the experiment and the experimenters acting as observers. Divorces, job losses, suicides, lack of funding, or blatant frauds end up ruining years of inquiry. It’s one thing to study traditional or religious groups that have some element of the paranormal in their practice; it’s a totally different thing to study it within the context of everyday life, where there are no bounds to the experience and no set structure for how it should be interpreted. The fringe nature of paranormal phenomena plays a part in this to some extent. Many of the people drawn to studying them or who are themselves being studied are already on the fringes of society. Having stepped outside of the norm, they are ripe for destabilizing events and not always ready for their consequence. Those who begin to study these phenomena with solid beliefs already in place are either shaken to the point that they become “true believers,” dropping all scientific credibility and distancing themselves from what is considered legitimate research, or they become rigid debunkers to protect their own sanity and credibility. This is a common element in any situation in which a crisis in belief leads to a breakdown of binary thinking. William Sargant’s work on possession and trance states shows how extreme emotional states can lead to transpersonal experiences. In many studies nothing happens at all, or the results are not repeatable, making it impossible to gather a baseline of data for reasonable comparison. Even in cases where laboratory tests seem to indicate positive effects, at one point or another some bit of trickery or lax research controls seem to emerge in place of legitimate ability. In traditional cultures the use of sleight of hand and showmanship is well known as part of the shamanic repertoire. To the Western mind this appears as fraudulent; traditional healers, however, will explain the necessity of such tricks and techniques to distract and engage the spirits. Even when sleight of hand is apparent in anomalous phenomena, the interplay of events often shows that there is something more going on, but to justify this in the laboratory, under strict scientific methodology, is impossible. SALIENT SLEIGHT OF HAND One of the most common means used to debunk claims of the paranormal is a thorough knowledge of the art of illusion, stage magic, and the psychologically potent tricks of mentalism. Like the progression of many arts the art of legerdemain and illusion suffers a slow drift from origins as a spiritual technique into an abused form of popular entertainment and a malignant tool in the hands of skeptics. What happens if we take Keel’s approach to the tools of a hoax, and understand its potency in creating real effects? What happens when the tools of the debunker are admitted, but instead of being bitterly thrust forward to expose the immediate hoax, they are rather pushed further to reveal more profound mysteries? It’s a mistake to think that mystery disappears when one understands the illusion. Theater, poetry, sculpture, painting, all forms of artifice have their basis in ritual. As Rachael Blyth, of the ritualtheater ensemble Foolish People, points out in her exploration of ritual media “Inside the Temple of Cinema,” even modified by contemporary technology, the sacred root of art remains approachable to those who know where to find it. The same goes for a good story, for an unexplainable moment of experience can only find reality when couched in a strange intermixture of material fact and imaginal proposition. Philosophy, when fully expressed, encompasses all aspects of life. A relationship with wisdom provides the basis for everything that follows, and properly aligned, all experience and action come under its guidance. Something such as sleight of hand, the ability to manipulate perception, is a powerful spiritual technique when given the right spin. It does well enough as a tool for skeptics to peel back the layers of mystery, but why stop at the flesh when you can reach well beyond the heart. Laboratory results in experiments with Y (psi, a term used for a broad range of paranormal phenomena) often reveal a very interesting form of the observer effect, demonstrating correlations between positive reinforcement and belief and gaining results. This can be observed in the placebo effect as well, where belief becomes, in some ways, the basic motivating factor of success. This is often used to counter miraculous claims without ever noticing that, in themselves, both of these tendencies are a bit miraculous. ILLUMINATION AND THE PROPER USE OF STAGE MAGIC When seeking an explanation for phenomena, or a straight materialist cause/effect scenario, the observer effect and placebos don’t provide an adequate basis for a proper scientific model. However, if we seek to understand and utilize these factors in our lives, no model is necessary, merely a grasp of the situational and atmospheric requirements to induce the phenomenon. The fact that devotees of Elvis report miraculous healing shows the power of a charismatic stage presence goes a long way. When that presence is guided by sound philosophy, this power can be used to move beyond selling albums into more refined realms of social change. Imagine the ability for a full expression of art, one that moves beyond the separate practices of visual arts, music, poetry, and dance, and that combines them with a mastery of perception. THE MIRACLE OF SUGAR During the early twentieth century, Harlan Tarbell, a close friend of Harry Houdini, developed one of the most enduring courses on stage magic. A unique aspect of Tarbell’s course was its focus on physical exercises to encourage greater body control. Similar to preset routines practiced in the martial arts or ballet, these exercises prepare students for future situations and allow muscle memory to take over when the magician’s focus is required elsewhere to direct his or her performance. As with any repeated physical exercise, these routines also aid in a deeper understanding of our interconnection between body and mind, and if extended further, of the spirit. In some ways Tarbell endorsed a sort of yoga for practicing illusionists. It is interesting to note accounts from a visit Tarbell made shortly after World War I to Le Mesnil-Saint-Denis in the north of France: A few years ago, Cagliostro [i.e., Tarbell] came to Husson Castle. He was a stranger, nobody had seen him before, nor knew exactly where he came from and where he went. But he did wonders. One day, Madame Husson had guests for dinner and she said she regretted not being able to serve the sugar with the coffee. Then this strange man, Cagliostro, got up and found in the air, enough sugar for all of the guests. Then he went to the monastery and had compassion for the nuns, orphans and wounded, he raised his hands and produced sugar. Then a hundred pounds of sugar arrived so they were able to retain all the results. The sugar appeared in the air, sir, materialized out of the air. Yes, sir, materialized out of the air. Yes, sir, we saw a miracle. 6 As master magician Jeff McBride teaches, the principles of stage magic require a thorough knowledge of optics, psychology, body control, set, and setting. To perform sleight of hand one must truly be on intimate terms with the ways in which the physical world interacts with the mind. Going farther with this, one could say that to achieve truly lasting results, one must have mastered the very illusion of the world that surrounds us. McBride puts it succinctly when he says, “To make magic, one has to have experienced magic.” One who explores the art of perception gains the ability to create effects in the world by altering the perception of witnesses. Thinking of a stage magician in this way may seem grandiose, but the use that this knowledge has played in debunking fraud and exposing hoaxes shows that such an understanding can be used as a powerful tool for exposing the truth. But as much as it can expose, it can also foster a connection to the truth. When we think of the full potential of this, it becomes obvious that the path of legerdemain leads into very interesting spiritual territory. BITTER MISDIRECTION Fundamentalist skepticism has kept this understanding of altering the perception of witnesses from reaching its full development as a cultural tool. When accounts of traditional healers using sleight of hand are retold, it is usually with disdain for the practice. Similarly, when we think of sleight of hand, it’s often in the context of annoying party tricks or an older relative whose abusive coin materialization lost its charm on the hundredth go-round. Gustavo Rol, an Italian enigma whose admirers included the filmmaker Federico Fellini, stated that the purpose of his “abilities” was to foster belief in something greater. Rol’s psychical demonstrations have provided ample fodder for skeptics, but what is continuously missed is the atmosphere of possibility that his demonstrations engendered. The storyteller brings the story to life through controlling an audience’s perception. This can encompass the entire spectrum of perception and needn’t include only words and sounds. By using sleight of hand and other techniques to induce phenomena, the audience is not only allowed to imagine the possibilities of the story, but to engage with them directly as they break into mundane reality through materialization, precognitive revelations, and other outré events. Whether this is done through a magic trick or through innate ability, the result in the life of the observer is the same unless they choose to discount their feelings, because of some skeptical sense of propriety. THESE ARE THE GODS YE WORSHIP Within mainstream religious groups we can see traces of this connection between illumination and illusion. There is the austere Moses of the Judeo-Christian tradition, but there is also Moses the Conjurer from the African diaspora traditions. The “Hoodoo” Moses gains his abilities not through some vague notion of divine intervention, but through a practical connection to divine wisdom that works on all levels of reality. In the apocryphal text Bel and the Dragon, we find the prophet Daniel playing a part very similar to a psychical investigator: And in that same place there was a great dragon, which they of Babylon worshipped. And the king said unto Daniel, Wilt thou also say that this is of brass? Lo, he liveth, he eateth and drinketh; thou canst not say that he is no living god: therefore worship him. Then said Daniel unto the king, I will worship the Lord my God: for he is the living God. But give me leave, O king, and I shall slay this dragon without sword or staff. The king said, I give thee leave. Then Daniel took pitch, and fat, and hair, and did seethe them together, and made lumps thereof: this he put in the dragon’s mouth, and so the dragon burst in sunder: and Daniel said, Lo, these are the gods ye worship. (23–27) There is no mysterious deus ex machina here outside of the dragon itself. Daniel uses an understanding of mechanics and incendiary devices to expose the fraud, without relying on visionary states to prove the deception. Earlier in the text when he is confronted with Bel, another construct that uses innovative manipulations to “eat” its offerings, he spreads ashes on the floor to expose footprints leading to hidden doors in the temple used to produce the effect. We must not forget, however, Daniel is still accounted a prophet and interpreter of dreams. If we are able to approach this story with the full spectrum of Daniel’s character in mind, the trickery lies not on the part of strange movements in reality, but the mechanical means by which a corrupt priestly class has manipulated the beliefs in such movements to satiate their own greed. BEYOND DIVISIONS Tarbell’s miracle amounts to very little in physical fact, the appearance of sugar, but this unique manifestation of an everyday item, aided by an illusionist’s skill, opens up the participants to a whole new view of reality for the observer. The miracle is not in the manifestation, but in the corresponding incidence of experiential rings: the impoverished state of the area, Tarbell’s appearance, and the events that followed. Master magician Eugene Burger has written extensively on how some of the most common magic tricks explore greater truths in profound ways. One example that Burger gives is the Ring Trick, in which rings are manipulated to appear as if they are able to join and disjoin without breaking the circle of any one ring. As a common trick, this has been used for centuries; Burger, however, points out that it also resonates deeply with the nature of reality: separate parts uniting in a whole while maintaining their individual divisions. The rejection of sleight of hand and the various techniques of stage magic as legitimate tools for revelation represents a failure of the dualistic, either/or mindset embedded in our culture. We are led to reject what we experience when the means of that experience are shown to be something unexpected. A small moment of possibility, one that could open up a lifetime of deeper union with reality, is thrown out by our antagonism toward the methods used to invoke it. As a tool for engendering the ground of belief these techniques provide unique opportunities to encourage the sense of mystery necessary for the opening of any spiritual quest. Beyond illusory phenomena lies the root of truth, and confronting these illusions in an active way we gain a deeper understanding of ourselves and the world we share. Paranthropology is an attempt to approach these events from another angle, one in which there are different measurements for credibility. This is closer to the phenomenological approach recommended by philosopher Joseph M. Felser in his essay “Philosophical Sensitives and Sensitive Philosophers: Gazing into the Future of Parapsychology.” Felser recognizes the inherent difficulties of fitting anomalous phenomena into any set theoretical structure. Rather than trying to explain them through predetermined theories or fit them into dogmatic beliefs, he sees a value in exploring the phenomena experientially. AS MUCH ABOUT MEANING AS MATTER This is also the approach that Jeffrey Kripal, J. Newton Rayzor Professor of Religious Studies and Chair of the Department of Religious Studies at Rice University, takes in his recent work Authors of the Impossible . As he says on the website for the documentary based on the book: “The paranormal, it turns out, is as much about meaning as matter. And we—not as surface egos, but as some still mysterious force of consciousness—are its final authors. If the paranormal, though, is as much about meaning as matter, as much about the subject as the object, then science can never truly grasp it, for science must turn everything into an object and cannot treat questions of meaning. We thus need a new way of knowing, a way that can embrace both the sciences and a new art of reading ourselves writing ourselves.” Whether or not they are provable in a laboratory setting, anomalous experiences remain a part of life for a surprising number of people. Gallup polls show that 17 percent of the population in the United States claim to have had a UFO experience. The Baylor Religion Survey, as detailed in Paranormal America , by Christopher Bader, F. Carson Mencken, and Joseph O. Baker shows that in the United States, 45 percent of women and 32 percent of men believe in the existence of ghosts, and 31 percent of women and 28 percent of men believe in telekinesis. If you take into account the various categories of paranormal possibilities, close to half of the population believes in, or claims to have experienced, something out of the ordinary in their lives. These statistics show that the paranormal is a significant motivating factor in how our culture develops and understands itself. The hope fostered in the formation of the Society for Psychical Research was that by investigating these experiences we would be lead to a deeper understanding of ourselves and our place in the world. Ultimately the secrets of anomalous experiences, whether they are purely psychosomatic or are based in material fact, go right to the heart of the secrets of human experience itself. A SOCIETY CONSCIOUS OF THE TRUTH Any sufficiently rigorously defined magic is indistinguishable from technology. —Larry Niven The seventeenth-century philosopher Thomas Vaughan said, “it cannot be thought unreasonable and certainly not unseasonable, if a Society conscious of the Truth, and skilled in the abstruse principles of Nature, shall endeavor to rectify the world: for hitherto we have been abused with Greek Fables and a pretended knowledge of Causes, but without their much desired Effects.” Although the root causes of such phenomena may remain sub rosa, and unattainable by the prying eyes of science and skepticism, those who would approach them in humility, without prejudice, and with a narrative eye for meaning, will find much to explore. The applied mathematician Peter Rothman, who helped developed some of the first streaming media applications for sites such as www.Live365.com , has pointed out that while the futurist and author Arthur C. Clarke’s observation that advanced science begins to look like magic has been hammered into the collective consciousness, there is a flip side to the equation, one articulated by author Larry Niven, which is equally valid. Niven proposed that those things that are considered magical, anomalous, or paranormal are themselves, when properly understood, a subject of true science that takes into account experiential truths whose effects are observable, and under the right conditions repeatable. Repeatability is not a problem when it comes to anomalous experiences. We’ve all had them, whether it’s a synchronicity in repeating numbers, a brief precognitive burst, or some more profound encounter with the other order. In seeking to fit reality to our assumptions, we fall into the dilemma that Vaughan points out, “we have been abused with Greek Fables and a pretended knowledge of Causes, but without their much desired Effects.” EVERYTHING IS TURNED UPSIDE DOWN And when they found them not; they drew Jason and certain brethren unto the rulers of the city, crying, These that have turned the world upside down are come hither. —Acts 17:6 In the magical world everything is turned upside down; the Greek fables that Vaughan mentions are not the rites of Dionysus, the Mysteries of Demeter and Persephone, or Socrates’s daimon, they are the same faulty methods of Galen, Hippocrates, and the other founders of a crippled science so widely denounced by Giordano Bruno, William Blake, and, sadly, by the very methodology of science itself. It is the fable of mathematics divorced from mysticism. We needn’t fall into the error of the skeptic, using tricks of rhetoric and technique to manipulate an exposition that provides false proof of a logical error, observation alone shows that there is more going on with anomalous phenomena than the madness of crowds or personal delusion. As Rothman suggests, our future science is not founded on increasing the cleverness of our automatons, rather it is a relationship with a most profound and personal magic. The ground is already laid for a generation of creative, scientific philosophers to move beyond stalemate arguments. Keel and other active explorers of the theoretical unknown have laid a vast groundwork. We have our critical theory, what we need now is our new Archimedes, whose mathematical models of infinity were recently discovered hidden beneath the devout prayers of the faithful. What we need now are those who will cease dead-end debates and join the active building of a new era of conscious engagement with the mystery of life. What we need now are those who will show up, silhouettes against the sky on a rainy night, and risk being mistaken for the devil himself.
